missed: Lewis expected the hardest part of being a first-time CEO to be the sheer number of hours required, since he'd already worked comparably long hours at management consulting firms and big tech companies. What actually turned out to be different - and harder - wasn't time spent but the inability to mentally disengage: as CEO he found he was "never not thinking" about the company, even during time nominally spent with his family. His model shifted from viewing work-life balance as a time-allocation problem to viewing it as an attention-and-mental-processing problem that hours worked don't capture.

In explaining Convoy's "know why" value, Lewis makes a specific quantitative argument for why transparency about reasoning matters at scale: he estimates that in a large company like Microsoft, perhaps 10% of employees are the deeply engaged, "diehard" contributors who are two-to-ten times more valuable than disengaged employees who are "just there to get paid," and that moving that ratio from 10% to just 15% would be transformational for the company's overall productivity. This specific hypothetical percentage shift is his justification for building a value around explaining the "why" behind decisions, so employees can exercise independent judgment rather than following instructions blindly.
net_new_genuine: Lewis found that the specific value of his six weeks of guerrilla customer research wasn't the data itself — he explicitly says he "didn't have data yet" — but the anecdotes it gave him. Having a first-hand story ready for every question an investor could ask shifted his pitch from "I have an idea" to a narrative backed by real conversations, and he credits this shift with changing the substance of his fundraising meetings, not just their polish.

As a concrete marker of Convoy's early trajectory, Lewis states that in roughly its first year as a real company the business grew to about 45 employees and raised approximately $19 million in funding — figures he offers as evidence of how compressed and fast his transition into the CEO role actually was, tying the pace of headcount and capital growth directly to how disorienting the experience felt.

Building Convoy's MVP took about four months end-to-end — the engineering team was assembled in April/May and the product launched with real customers between the end of August and beginning of September of the same year. Lewis's dominant fear throughout that build wasn't generic startup anxiety but a specific one: that after months of engineering work nobody would actually use the product, and that everyone who told him during development that they needed it had just been being polite. That fear only resolved the moment their actual first customer — Guided Products, a Kent, WA-based printed-materials company — completed its first real shipment on the platform (a couple of pallets of paper moved between two Puget Sound facilities).

While racing to ship Convoy's MVP in just four months, one of the engineers, Alex, pushed the team with a line Dan still quotes: get something out the door so they could actually learn what they should be building, rather than trying to design it perfectly upfront. That instinct paid off unusually well - the end-to-end, self-service, fully automated version they shipped turned out to be close to the right product, and roughly two and a half months after launch they were able to close their seed fundraise on the strength of the traction it generated. Dan attributes the low-risk outcome not to luck but to the amount of field research (data, numbers, direct customer conversations) that had already been done before the MVP was built, even while acknowledging real risk still remained.

Before co-founding Convoy, Lewis engineered a deliberate, multi-year career path specifically to earn technical credibility despite starting in management consulting rather than engineering. He describes roughly five or six sequential job moves — consulting to business development at a startup (which bought him startup credibility), to product management, to a role working directly with product engineering, to eventually managing engineering and product — each move calculated to close the gap toward a known end-state: enough credibility to be trusted by a technical co-founder and to be able to hire engineers himself. This is a specific, intentional career-sequencing strategy aimed at a predetermined future goal, not a generic "get more experience" narrative.

Lewis said working with investors hasn't been hard for him personally, but frames the relationship with a specific and somewhat counterintuitive warning: the moment they sign the first check is essentially the high point of the relationship, and it tends to get harder from there, not easier, once real difficulty hits. Because of that trajectory, he treats picking investors like dating, prioritizing genuine chemistry and the ability to have an open conversation during the easy early period, precisely because that same rapport gets tested later when things aren't going well. This runs counter to the assumption that investor relationships settle into smoother cooperation as trust builds over time — his experience is the reverse: the initial rapport is the peak, and everything after is harder until, maybe, a payoff moment much later.
